---
title: "Strategize Package Tutorial"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Strategize Package Tutorial}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\usepackage[UTF-8]{inputenc}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
CONDA_AVAILABLE <- FALSE

if (identical(Sys.getenv("STRATEGIZE_RUN_VIGNETTE"), "true")) {
  CONDA_AVAILABLE <- tryCatch({
    if (requireNamespace("reticulate", quietly = TRUE)) {
      conda_list <- reticulate::conda_list()
      "strategize_env" %in% conda_list$name
    } else {
      FALSE
    }
  }, error = function(e) FALSE)
}

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  eval = CONDA_AVAILABLE
)

if (!CONDA_AVAILABLE && interactive()) {
  message(
    "Note: vignette code chunks are not executed by default. ",
    "To run them, set STRATEGIZE_RUN_VIGNETTE=true and make sure ",
    "the 'strategize_env' conda environment exists."
  )
}
```

# Introduction

`strategize` learns probability distributions over conjoint factor levels that optimize a chosen outcome. In the standard workflow, the package fits an outcome model from observed conjoint data and then searches for the factor-level distribution that performs best under that fitted model. In adversarial mode, the package learns a pair of distributions that respond strategically to one another.

This vignette focuses on the core single-study workflow:

- preparing pairwise conjoint data
- fitting an average-case optimization with `cv_strategize()`
- fitting an adversarial optimization with `strategize(adversarial = TRUE)`
- inspecting the returned distributions and outcome summaries

For prediction-only modeling, see `?strategic_prediction`. For foundation-model runtime use of trained `preference.fm` checkpoints, see `vignette("FoundationModels")`.

## Installation and Backend Setup

Install the package from GitHub and create the JAX-backed computational environment once:

```r
# remotes::install_github("cjerzak/strategize-software/strategize")
library(strategize)
strategize::build_backend(conda_env = "strategize_env")
```

The `strategize_env` backend is used by the optimization workflow in `strategize()` and `cv_strategize()`, and by the package's neural prediction and foundation-model APIs.

# Constructing Example Data

Most `strategize` analyses need:

- `Y`: an observed outcome vector
- `W`: a factor matrix or data frame
- `p_list`: the baseline factor-level probabilities
- pairwise identifiers such as `pair_id` and `profile_order` for forced-choice designs

The example below simulates a simple pairwise conjoint with three factors and one observed winner per pair.

```{r example-data}
library(strategize)

set.seed(123)
n_pairs <- 500

candidate_left <- data.frame(
  Sex = sample(c("Male", "Female"), n_pairs, replace = TRUE),
  Age = sample(c("36-51", "52-67", "68-76"), n_pairs, replace = TRUE),
  Family = sample(
    c(
      "Single (divorced)",
      "Single (never married)",
      "Married (no child)",
      "Married (two children)"
    ),
    n_pairs,
    replace = TRUE
  )
)

candidate_right <- data.frame(
  Sex = sample(c("Male", "Female"), n_pairs, replace = TRUE),
  Age = sample(c("36-51", "52-67", "68-76"), n_pairs, replace = TRUE),
  Family = sample(
    c(
      "Single (divorced)",
      "Single (never married)",
      "Married (no child)",
      "Married (two children)"
    ),
    n_pairs,
    replace = TRUE
  )
)

utility_left <- 0.20 * (candidate_left$Sex == "Female") +
  0.10 * (candidate_left$Age == "36-51") +
  0.05 * (candidate_left$Family == "Married (two children)")

utility_right <- 0.20 * (candidate_right$Sex == "Female") +
  0.10 * (candidate_right$Age == "36-51") +
  0.05 * (candidate_right$Family == "Married (two children)")

Y_left <- as.numeric(utility_left > utility_right)
Yobs <- c(Y_left, 1 - Y_left)

FACTOR_MAT_ <- rbind(candidate_left, candidate_right)
pair_id <- c(seq_len(n_pairs), seq_len(n_pairs))
respondent_id <- pair_id
respondent_task_id <- pair_id
profile_order <- c(rep(1L, n_pairs), rep(2L, n_pairs))

p_list <- create_p_list(FACTOR_MAT_, uniform = TRUE)
cluster_var <- respondent_id
```

# Average-Case Optimization

Use `cv_strategize()` when you want cross-validation over `lambda`. The result object uses `pi_star_point`, `pi_star_se`, `Q_point`, and `Q_se` as the primary public outputs, and this vignette uses those canonical field names throughout.

```{r average-case}
res_avecase <- cv_strategize(
  Y = Yobs,
  W = FACTOR_MAT_,
  lambda_seq = c(0.01, 0.1, 0.5),
  penalty_type = "KL",
  pair_id = pair_id,
  respondent_id = respondent_id,
  respondent_task_id = respondent_task_id,
  profile_order = profile_order,
  p_list = p_list,
  varcov_cluster_variable = cluster_var,
  diff = TRUE,
  nSGD = 100,
  compute_se = TRUE,
  folds = 3,
  conda_env = "strategize_env",
  conda_env_required = TRUE
)

res_avecase$pi_star_point
res_avecase$Q_point
res_avecase$Q_se
```

### Plotting the Learned Distribution

```{r plotting}
strategize.plot(
  pi_star_list = res_avecase$pi_star_point,
  pi_star_se_list = res_avecase$pi_star_se,
  p_list = p_list,
  main_title = "Average-case optimal distribution"
)
```

Interpret `pi_star_point` as the recommended reweighting of factor levels relative to the baseline design encoded in `p_list`. `Q_point` is the expected outcome under that learned distribution, and `Q_se` is its associated uncertainty when `compute_se = TRUE`.

# Adversarial Optimization

Set `adversarial = TRUE` when each side should optimize strategically against the other. The example below creates simple party labels for respondents and profiles and then fits the adversarial problem directly with `strategize()`.

```{r adversarial}
respondent_party <- ifelse(seq_len(n_pairs) %% 2L == 0L, "PartyA", "PartyB")
competing_group_variable_respondent <- respondent_party[respondent_id]

competing_group_variable_candidate <- ifelse(
  profile_order == 1L,
  "PartyA",
  "PartyB"
)

competing_group_competition_variable_candidate <- rep("Different", length(Yobs))

res_adversarial <- strategize(
  Y = Yobs,
  W = FACTOR_MAT_,
  lambda = 0.1,
  penalty_type = "KL",
  pair_id = pair_id,
  respondent_id = respondent_id,
  respondent_task_id = respondent_task_id,
  profile_order = profile_order,
  p_list = p_list,
  varcov_cluster_variable = cluster_var,
  competing_group_variable_respondent = competing_group_variable_respondent,
  competing_group_variable_candidate = competing_group_variable_candidate,
  competing_group_competition_variable_candidate = competing_group_competition_variable_candidate,
  diff = TRUE,
  adversarial = TRUE,
  nSGD = 100,
  compute_se = TRUE,
  conda_env = "strategize_env",
  conda_env_required = TRUE
)

res_adversarial$pi_star_point$k1
res_adversarial$pi_star_point$k2
res_adversarial$Q_point
res_adversarial$Q_se
```

In adversarial mode, `pi_star_point$k1` and `pi_star_point$k2` hold the two learned strategies. The corresponding `Q_point` value summarizes the equilibrium outcome under those strategies.

# Multi-Cluster Extension

If you want cluster-specific optimal distributions, provide respondent covariates in `X` and set `K > 1`. This asks the package to estimate multiple cluster-level solutions instead of a single pooled distribution.

```{r cluster-based}
set.seed(321)
X <- cbind(
  ideology = rnorm(length(unique(respondent_id))),
  engagement = runif(length(unique(respondent_id)))
)
X <- X[respondent_id, , drop = FALSE]

res_clust <- cv_strategize(
  Y = Yobs,
  W = FACTOR_MAT_,
  X = X,
  lambda_seq = c(0.01, 0.1),
  p_list = p_list,
  pair_id = pair_id,
  respondent_id = respondent_id,
  respondent_task_id = respondent_task_id,
  profile_order = profile_order,
  diff = TRUE,
  K = 2L,
  nSGD = 100,
  compute_se = TRUE,
  conda_env = "strategize_env",
  conda_env_required = TRUE
)

res_clust$pi_star_point
res_clust$Q_point
res_clust$Q_se
```

# Other Public Workflows

The package now exposes additional public workflows that are separate from the main optimization path:

- `strategic_prediction()` fits the package outcome model without running intervention optimization.
- `load_conjoint_foundation_bundle()`, `adapt_conjoint_foundation_model()`, and `extract_embeddings()` consume trained foundation-model checkpoints from `preference.fm`.

Those workflows are documented separately so this tutorial can stay focused on the core single-study optimization path.

## References

Jerzak, Connor T., Priyanshi Chandra, and Rishi Hazra. 2025. "Selecting Optimal Candidate Profiles in Adversarial Environments Using Conjoint Analysis and Machine Learning." *arXiv preprint* arXiv:2504.19043. https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19043.

```bibtex
@misc{jerzak2025selectingoptimalcandidateprofiles,
      title={Selecting Optimal Candidate Profiles in Adversarial Environments Using Conjoint Analysis and Machine Learning},
      author={Connor T. Jerzak and Priyanshi Chandra and Rishi Hazra},
      year={2025},
      eprint={2504.19043},
      archivePrefix={arXiv},
      primaryClass={stat.ME},
      url={https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19043},
}
```
